msgs.text: This proof gives presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group. Is it correct?

**I would not yet regard it as a verified proof.** I found no immediate contradiction in the elementary consistency checks: the tame quotient is right, the maximal pro-\(2\) quotient is Nielsen-equivalent to the standard rank-three dyadic Demushkin presentation, the abelianization has the expected shape, and the displayed mod-\(2\) cup-product matrix agrees with the Hilbert pairing. Labute’s classification supports the Demushkin normal form used in Section 3. 

The unresolved, load-bearing part is Sections 6–9. In particular, the chain
\[
(87)\;\Longrightarrow\;(96)\;\Longrightarrow\;(97)
\;\Longrightarrow\;\text{Lemma 6.16}
\;\Longrightarrow\;\text{Proposition 6.17}
\]
identifies a particular normalized graph cocycle with the Evens norm, transports it through Shapiro and corestriction at cochain level, and proves its vanishing on deep dyadic units. Those normalization-sensitive assertions are given only compressed derivations. Proposition 6.17—the equality of the candidate and local determinant Gauss sums—then supplies the essential input to the Fourier-induction argument. Without an independent, detailed verification of this chain, the final surjection-count comparison is not established. The ramified Arf-invariant computation in Proposition 6.8 is similarly condensed and needs a complete representation-theoretic proof. 

The manuscript also says that the computational verification of the final word is not reproduced. That does not logically invalidate the claimed proof, since the computation is said not to be used, but it means the exact relator cannot presently be independently audited from the supplied material.

This would solve a problem still publicly listed as unsolved, so the appropriate conclusion is: **promising and internally sophisticated, but not presently certified as correct; neither have I found a short decisive counterexample.**
